images.caption: The note derives the magnitude and phase of H(s)=K/(s+B).
The numerical example H(s)=2/(s+2) is sketched as a low-pass response.

visuals.title: First-Order Magnitude Response
visuals.description: The single-pole magnitude is largest at low frequency and decays as omega increases.

visuals.title: First-Order Phase Response
visuals.description: The phase shifts gradually from 0 degrees toward -90 degrees.

visuals.title: Lecture Note: 3 dB Bandwidth
visuals.caption: The note shows that omega=B corresponds to a 3 dB drop from the low-frequency magnitude level.

visuals.title: Lecture Note: H(s)=2/(s+2)
visuals.caption: The example labels the pass band, stop band, and low-pass character for the case K=B=2.
